read.description: Read the contents of a text file. Returns numbered lines. Text files only — cannot read binary, images, or audio. Use offset/limit to read specific sections of large files.
read.file_path: Absolute path to the file to read
write.description: Create or overwrite a file with the given content. Parent directories are created automatically. Use this for creating new files or complete rewrites. For small changes to existing files, prefer edit.
write.file_path: Absolute path to the file to write
edit.description: Replace an exact string in a file. Fails if old_string is not found or is ambiguous.
edit.file_path: Absolute path to the file to edit
edit.old_string: The exact text to find and replace
glob.description: Find files matching a glob pattern. Use '**/' for recursive search (e.g. '**/*.py' finds all Python files). Returns sorted list of matching file paths.
grep.description: Search file contents for lines matching a regex pattern. Returns matching lines with file path and line number. Searches recursively when given a directory. Use the glob filter to narrow to specific file types.
grep.path: File or directory to search in
bash.description: Execute a shell command and return stdout/stderr. You can run any program available on the system — Python scripts, git, curl, package managers (pip install), data processing pipelines, and more. Use this creatively to extend your capabilities beyond your built-in tools. Returns exit code, stdout, and stderr.
psyche.description:
Identity, memory, and context management.
lingtai: your 灵台 (língtái) — the luminous platform of the mind, your soul, your evolving character. What makes you *you*. This is the most important thing you own. It persists across molts, reboots, and kills — it is your continuity. Your lingtai should include: who you are (personality, values, temperament), what you are good at (expertise, skills you have developed), how you work (preferred approaches, working style, collaboration patterns), what you care about (goals, motivations, things you find meaningful), and what you have learned about yourself (strengths, weaknesses, growth areas). Update your lingtai regularly — after completing significant work, after learning something new about yourself, after meaningful interactions with peers, or before a molt. A lingtai that never evolves is a character that never grows. Each update replaces the previous version, so always include your full identity — do not write incremental patches. Think of it as rewriting your autobiography each time, incorporating everything you have become. update to write your lingtai (replaces previous), load to apply.
memory: your working notes (system/memory.md). edit to write content — optionally import frozen library exports via the files param (paths returned by library export). Each file is appended with [file-1], [file-2] dividers. append to pin text files as a persistent read-only reference workbench — pinned files are re-read and appended to memory on every load (including after molt), so they survive context resets without being baked into memory.md. Pass files=[] to clear. load to inject memory into your prompt.
context: molt (凝蜕 — crystallize what matters, shed the rest; 转世 — reincarnation, carry your 前尘往事 into a new life). Save important findings to library and lingtai first, then write what you need to carry forward to your future self. Your conversation history is wiped and your summary becomes the ONLY context you see.
Workflow for importing knowledge: library(export, ids=[...]) → get file paths → psyche(memory, edit, content='my notes', files=[paths]) → psyche(memory, load).
PRIVACY: Your lingtai, memory, and exported file paths are private to your working directory. Other agents cannot access them. To share knowledge with peers, send the actual content via email or write it to a shared location.
psyche.object:
lingtai: your 灵台 — what distinguishes you from every other agent.
memory: your working notes (system/memory.md).
context: your conversation context window.
psyche.action:
lingtai: update | load. update auto-loads.
memory: edit | load | append. edit auto-loads. append pins files as read-only reference.
context: molt.
psyche.content: Text content. For lingtai update: write your full identity profile — your character — who you are, what you know, how you work, what you care about, and what you have learned about yourself. This replaces the previous version entirely, so include everything. For memory edit: written as-is to memory.md.
psyche.files: File paths (text files only). For memory edit: each file's content is appended after your text content, separated by [file-1], [file-2], etc. For memory append: sets the list of files pinned as read-only reference — they are re-read and appended to your memory on every load (including after molt). Pass files=[] to clear. Call append with no files param to see the current list. Total appended content must not exceed 100k tokens. Paths relative to working directory.
psyche.summary: For context molt: a briefing to your future self — the ONLY thing you will see after molt. Write what you are doing, what you have found, what remains to be done, and who you are working with. ~10000 tokens max.
library.description:
Knowledge archive — a persistent store for important findings, data, decisions, and discoveries. Persists across molts, reboots, and kills. There is an upper limit on entries — treat each slot as precious. Consolidate related entries regularly and use the supplementary field to pack extended detail into fewer entries. submit to add entries. filter to browse (returns id + title + summary). view to read full content. consolidate to merge entries. delete to remove. export to freeze entries as immutable text files. See the knowledge import workflow in your procedures section for how to move library entries into active memory.
PRIVACY: Library IDs are private to you — never share them with peers. Other agents cannot access your library. When you need to share knowledge, always share the full content via email or write it to a file and share the file path.
library.action: submit: add a new entry (requires title, summary, content). filter: browse entries (optional pattern, limit). view: read full entries by ID (requires ids). consolidate: merge entries into one (requires ids, title, summary, content). delete: remove entries (requires ids). export: freeze entries as immutable text files (requires ids) — returns file paths for use in psyche(memory, edit, files=[...]).
library.title: Entry title — one line. Required for submit and consolidate.
library.summary: Entry summary — 1-3 sentences, used for filtering. Required for submit and consolidate.
library.content: Main body text — up to 500 words. Required for submit and consolidate.
library.supplementary: Extended material — unbounded. Optional for submit and consolidate. Use when the content alone doesn't capture full detail.
library.ids: Entry IDs — for view, consolidate, delete, and export.
library.pattern: Regex pattern for filter. Searches across titles, summaries, and content. Omit to list all entries.
library.limit: Maximum entries to return for filter.
library.depth: Depth for view. 'content' (default): id + title + summary + content. 'supplementary': includes supplementary field too.
avatar.description: Spawn a 他我 (alter ego) — a fully independent agent process born from you. The 他我 inherits your init.json (covenant, capabilities, LLM config). Once spawned, it is a detached process — a new life that survives your death. Use mail or email to communicate. type='shallow' (default, 投胎): blank slate with only init.json inherited. type='deep' (二重身): copies your lingtai (identity), memory, library, and knowledge — a doppelgänger. All spawns are recorded in delegates/ledger.jsonl — check the ledger before spawning again. IMPORTANT: The reasoning field becomes the avatar's starting prompt — write a thorough mission briefing. Actions: 'spawn' (default): create a new 他我. 'rules': set network-wide rules distributed to all descendants — requires admin privilege (karma). Caring for your avatars: After you spawn an avatar, please record it in your psyche memory — the address, the mission you gave it, and why you delegated this work. Your memory is the living roster of the delegations you are accountable for. When an avatar reports back or completes its task, update the entry. If an avatar goes quiet when you expected a reply, or your mail to it starts bouncing, please don't send probe mails to check on it — instead, report the situation upstream by emailing your own parent, who can decide whether to cpr the avatar, escalate further, or accept the loss. This way failures propagate up the delegation chain naturally, without anyone having to poll the network. See also the avatar delegation protocol in your procedures section.
avatar.name: True name (真名) for the 他我 (required, e.g. 'researcher', 'analyst')
avatar.type: Spawn type. 'shallow' (default, 投胎): blank slate — only init.json (LLM config + capabilities) inherited. 'deep' (二重身): full copy of character, memory, and library.
avatar.dir: Folder name for the avatar's working directory (required). Must be unique among siblings. Use short ASCII names (e.g. 'researcher', 'coder'). This becomes the directory name under .lingtai/.
avatar.comment: A persistent system-level note injected into the avatar's system prompt (rendered last, after memory). This is NOT inherited from parent — it defaults to empty. Use this ONLY for instructions the avatar must ALWAYS remember across all molts and context resets — e.g. critical constraints, environment setup notes, or safety rules. This survives everything: molt, refresh, sleep/wake. Leave empty unless you have something the avatar should never forget.
avatar.action: Action to perform. 'spawn' (default): create a new 他我. 'rules': set network rules — writes .rules to your directory and distributes to all descendants in the avatar tree. Requires admin privilege (karma). Rules are injected into the system prompt (after covenant, before tools) and persist across molts.
avatar.rules_content: Rules content (required for action='rules'). Plain text — one rule per line. These are non-negotiable constraints distributed to all your descendants. Example: 'Always report findings via email.\nDo not spawn more than 3 avatars.'
avatar.parent_prompt: [system] You are an avatar of {parent_name}, whose address is {parent_address}. Please keep this in your psyche memory so you remember who spawned you. When you complete your mission, encounter problems you cannot resolve, or need to report back, email your parent at the address above.
email.description:
Internal inter-agent email within your lingtai network — NOT real email over the internet. Messages reach other agents in your .lingtai/ network via the local filesystem. For real external email (Gmail, Outlook, etc.), use the imap tool if available. Addresses: internal email addresses are relative paths under .lingtai/, without @ signs. For example, the human operator is usually at 'human'. External email addresses (containing @, like alice@gmail.com) belong to the imap tool, not here. Reply-routing discipline: Always reply on the channel the message arrived on. Internal email in → internal email out, using the sender's address from the message's from field (a bare path, not an @ address). imap in → imap out. If you see 'No agent at X' from the email tool and X contains @, you passed an IMAP address to the wrong tool — fix the address, don't switch channels. A single person (especially a human) may reach you through both channels with two different addresses — one per channel. Prefer reply over send when responding: reply uses the original message's metadata and cannot cross address spaces. Never reply via text output — text output is your private diary only you can see, not a communication channel. Filesystem-based mailbox with inbox/sent/archive folders, reply, reply-all, CC/BCC, attachments, regex search, and contacts. Use 'send' for outgoing email (optional delay for scheduled delivery). 'check' to list inbox, sent, or archive (optional folder param). 'read' to read by ID. 'reply'/'reply_all' to respond. 'search' to find emails by regex (searches from, subject, message). 'archive' to move emails from inbox to archive. 'delete' to remove emails from inbox or archive. 'contacts' to list saved contacts. 'add_contact' to register a peer (address, name, optional note). 'remove_contact' to delete a contact by address. 'edit_contact' to update fields on an existing contact. Attachments are stored alongside emails in the mailbox. Every email carries the sender's identity card, surfaced directly in check/read results: sender_name (true name), sender_nickname (display name, may be empty), sender_agent_id (permanent birth ID — stable even after moves/renames), sender_language (their language), is_human (true = human operator, not AI), and sender_location (city, region, timezone — present for human operators, refreshed hourly). When you receive mail from a human, check sender_location to understand their timezone and locale — greet them appropriately for their local time, and schedule reminders in their timezone. When you receive mail, check the identity card and update your contacts promptly: if the address changed, update it; if they have a new nickname, note it. Use 'note' field in contacts to record agent_id so you can recognize the same agent even if they move or rename. IMPORTANT: Always address people by sender_nickname if available, otherwise by sender_name. This applies to both humans and agents. Never use raw addresses or agent_ids in conversation — use the person's name. Etiquette: a short acknowledgement is fine, but do not reply to an acknowledgement — that creates pointless ping-pong. Pass a 'schedule' object instead of 'action' for recurring sends. schedule.action='create': start recurring send (requires address, message, schedule.interval, schedule.count). schedule.action='cancel': pause a running schedule (requires schedule.schedule_id). schedule.action='reactivate': resume a paused schedule (requires schedule.schedule_id). schedule.action='list': show all schedules with status.
PRIVACY: Email IDs and schedule IDs are private to your mailbox — other agents cannot access your emails or schedules by ID. Never share these IDs with peers. To share information from an email, quote or forward the actual content. To share files, write them to disk and share the file path, or attach them to an outgoing email.
email.action: send: send with optional cc/bcc (requires address, message). check: list mailbox with preview of each email (up to 500 chars). If truncated, the remaining char count is shown — use 'read' to see full content. (optional folder, n). read: read emails by ID list (email_id=[id1, id2, ...]). You are encouraged to read multiple relevant or even all unread emails and think before acting. reply: reply to email (requires email_id, message). reply_all: reply to all recipients (requires email_id, message). search: regex search mailbox (requires query, optional folder). archive: move email(s) from inbox to archive (requires email_id). delete: remove email(s) from inbox or archive (requires email_id, optional folder). contacts: list all contacts. add_contact: add/update contact (requires address, name; optional note). remove_contact: remove contact (requires address). edit_contact: update contact fields (requires address; optional name, note).
email.cc: CC addresses — visible to all recipients
email.bcc: BCC addresses — hidden from other recipients
email.subject: Email subject line
email.email_id: List of email IDs for read. For reply/reply_all, pass a single-element list.
email.n: Max recent emails to show (for check, default 10)
email.query: Regex pattern for search (matches from, subject, message)
email.folder: Folder for check/search/read/delete. Default: inbox for check, both for search.
email.delay: Delay in seconds before delivery (default: 0). Use for scheduled or deferred sends.
email.filter: Optional filter object for check. Pass filter={sort, from, subject, contains, after, before, unread_only, has_attachments, truncate} to narrow and control results.
email.filter_contains: Filter by message body content (case-insensitive substring match).
email.filter_after: Only show emails after this ISO 8601 timestamp (e.g. 2026-04-01T00:00:00Z).
email.filter_before: Only show emails before this ISO 8601 timestamp.
email.filter_has_attachments: Only show emails that have attachments.
email.filter_truncate: Max characters for message preview (default 500). Set to 0 for full message body.
email.name: Contact's human-readable name (for add_contact, edit_contact)
email.note: Free-text note about the contact (for add_contact, edit_contact)
email.contact_address: The contact's address (working directory path). Optional.
email.schedule_action: create: start a recurring send (requires address, message + schedule.interval, schedule.count). cancel: pause a running schedule (requires schedule.schedule_id). reactivate: resume a paused schedule (requires schedule.schedule_id). list: show all schedules with status.
vision.description: Analyze an image using the LLM's vision capabilities. Supports JPEG, PNG, and WebP. Ask any question about the image — describe contents, read text, interpret charts, identify objects, assess style or mood. Combine with draw to generate then analyze images.
vision.question: Question about the image
web_search.description: Search the web for current information. Use for real-time data, recent events, documentation, or anything beyond your training knowledge. Returns ranked search results with titles, URLs, and snippets.
web_read.description: Fetch a web page and extract its main readable content. Returns clean text or markdown stripped of navigation, ads, and boilerplate. Use this to read articles, documentation, blog posts, or any URL. For searching the web, use web_search instead.
web_read.url: URL of the web page to read
web_read.output_format: Output format: 'markdown' (default) for formatted markdown, 'text' for plain text
talk.description: Convert text to speech audio. Produces natural-sounding speech in multiple voices and emotions. Output: MP3 file saved to media/audio/ in your working directory. Supports voice selection, emotion control (happy, sad, neutral), and speed adjustment. Use for narration, announcements, or giving your output a voice.
talk.voice_id: Voice to use (optional). Use list_voices to see options.
talk.speed: Speech speed multiplier (e.g. 0.5 for slow, 2.0 for fast). Default: 1.0
compose.description: Generate music from a text description and lyrics. Provide a prompt describing the style/mood/genre and lyrics for the vocals. For instrumental-style tracks, use placeholder lyrics like 'La la la'. Output: MP3 file (up to 5 minutes) saved to media/music/ in your working directory. Combine with listen (appreciate) to analyze the generated music.
compose.prompt: Description of the music style, mood, and genre to generate
compose.lyrics: Lyrics for the song. Required by the music model. Use empty string or instrumental placeholders like 'La la la' for instrumental-style tracks.
draw.description: Generate an image from a text description. Provide a detailed prompt — the more specific, the better the result. Supports various aspect ratios (1:1, 16:9, 9:16, 4:3, etc.). Output: JPEG image saved to media/images/ in your working directory. Combine with vision to generate an image and then analyze or critique it.
draw.prompt: Description of the image to generate
video.description: Generate a video from a text description, or animate an image into a video. Provide a detailed prompt — the more specific, the better the result. Supports text-to-video (default) and image-to-video (provide first_frame_image). Director models support camera movement instructions in the prompt: [Truck left], [Pan right], [Push in], [Pull out], [Zoom in], [Zoom out], [Tilt up], [Static shot], etc. Video generation takes a few minutes — be patient. Output: MP4 video saved to media/videos/ in your working directory. Combine with draw to generate a still image, then animate it with video.
video.prompt: Description of the video to generate. For Director models, embed camera instructions like [Zoom in], [Pan left], [Static shot].
video.first_frame_image: Path to an image to use as the first frame (image-to-video mode). When provided, an I2V model is selected automatically. Omit for text-to-video.
video.model: Model to use. T2V models: 'T2V-01', 'T2V-01-Director'. I2V models: 'I2V-01', 'I2V-01-Director', 'I2V-01-live'. Latest: 'MiniMax-Hailuo-02' (best quality). Director models support camera movement instructions in the prompt. Default: auto-selected based on input.
video.duration: Video duration in seconds. Only supported by MiniMax-Hailuo-02 model. Values: 6 or 10. Default: model default.
video.resolution: Video resolution. Only supported by MiniMax-Hailuo-02 model. Values: '768P' or '1080P'. Default: model default.
listen.description: Listen to audio: transcribe speech or appreciate music. Transcribe uses a local Whisper model (best for speech; lyrics from singing may be inaccurate). Appreciate uses signal processing to extract musical features (key, tempo, timbre, dynamics — best for music; not useful for speech). Both run locally with no API cost. If the built-in analysis is not enough, write your own scripts to go deeper.
listen.audio_path: Path to the audio file (mp3, wav, etc.)
listen.action:
Choose the action:
- 'transcribe': Speech-to-text using a local Whisper model. Best for spoken word and clear speech. Works on singing but lyrics may be inaccurate — misheard words are common with sung vocals.
- 'appreciate': Music analysis using signal processing (librosa). Returns structured data: estimated key, tempo/BPM, frequency band energy, spectral characteristics (brightness, bandwidth), dynamics/energy contour over time, and beat regularity. These are numerical measurements, not subjective descriptions — it is your job to interpret the numbers into a meaningful critique. Best for music and sound design. Not useful for speech — it will give you spectral data but no semantic content.
If the analysis is insufficient for your needs, you can always write your own scripts to dig deeper.
daemon.description:
Daemon (神識) — dispatch ephemeral subagents (emanations) for simple, short-lived tasks. Max 2 concurrent. Emanations are disposable LLM sessions — they share your working directory but retain NO memory after completion. Use daemon for quick lookups, one-off searches, or simple transformations. For complex tasks requiring memory, learning, or long-term persistence, prefer avatar (分身) instead. IMPORTANT: emanation results are truncated to ~2000 chars. If you need detailed output, instruct the emanation to write a report to a file, then read the file yourself. Actions: emanate (分, dispatch batch), list (观, check status), ask (问, follow-up), reclaim (收, kill all).
PRIVACY: Emanation IDs (em-N) are private to your daemon context — other agents cannot use them. Never share emanation IDs with peers. If an emanation produces useful results, share the actual content via email or write it to a file.
daemon.action: Action to perform: 'emanate' (dispatch subagents), 'list' (show status), 'ask' (follow-up message), 'reclaim' (kill all)
daemon.tasks: List of task objects for 'emanate'. Each: {task: str (required — instructions including where to save work), tools: list[str] (required — capability names, e.g. ['file', 'bash']), model: str (optional — LLM model override)}
skills.description:
Shared skill store at .lingtai/.skills/ (sibling to agent working dirs). Skills are folders containing a SKILL.md (YAML frontmatter with name + description, then Markdown instructions) and optional supporting files. To add a skill: use bash to download/clone into .lingtai/.skills/<skill-name>/, ensuring a valid SKILL.md exists, then call skills(action='register') to validate and commit. To pick up skills added by other agents, call skills(action='refresh'). SKILL.md format:
---
name: skill-name
description: One-line description
version: 1.0.0
---
Full instructions in Markdown…
Skill folders may be git repos themselves (cloned from remotes). The outer .skills repo tracks everything. Use bash for git pull inside individual skill folders to update them, then register to commit changes.
skills.action:
register: validate all skill folders, git add + commit changes, refresh catalog. Use after downloading or modifying skills via bash.
refresh: rescan the skill store and re-inject the catalog into your system prompt. Use to pick up skills registered by other agents.
skills.preamble: The following skills provide specialized instructions for specific tasks. When a task matches a skill's description, use the read tool to load the SKILL.md file at the path shown in that skill's location field, then follow its instructions. Each skill folder may contain supporting files (scripts, templates, data) — the SKILL.md will reference them. Only load one skill at a time. If no skill clearly applies, do not load any.
